problem.text: Given a sorted array of integers, find two numbers that add up to a target sum. Return their 1-indexed positions.
missingConstraints: Is there guaranteed to be exactly one solution?
Can the same element be used twice?

concepts.definition: When the array is sorted, if the current sum is too large, moving the right pointer left decreases it. If too small, moving the left pointer right increases it. This deterministic movement makes O(n) possible.
concepts.example:
[1, 3, 5, 7, 9]  target = 10
left=0(1), right=4(9) → sum=10 ✓
If sum too big  → right--
If sum too small → left++

easy_medium.explanation: O(n²) — the sorted property is unused. Two pointers achieve O(n).

easy_medium.text: Two pointers converging from both ends — adjust based on sum vs target
easy_medium.explanation: Sorted array + pair search = classic two-pointer. O(n) time, O(1) space.

easy_medium.text: Binary search for each element's complement
easy_medium.explanation: O(n log n) — works but misses the O(n) opportunity the sorted array provides.

cards.sayApproach: The array is sorted — that's the signal for two pointers. I place one at each end and check their sum. If the sum equals target, done. If too small, I move left right to increase it. If too large, I move right left to decrease it. O(n) time, O(1) space.

lines.narration: Pattern: two pointers at both ends

lines.text: if (sum == target) return new int[]{left+1, right+1};

lines.narration: Sum too small — move left right
Sum too large — move right left

code.verbalSummary: Classic two-pointer on a sorted array. Converge from both ends, adjusting based on whether the current sum is too small or too large. O(n) time, O(1) space. The sorted property is what makes this deterministic.
solRatingCriteria: Identified sorted array as the two-pointer signal
Explained the directional logic (too small → left++, too large → right--)
Stated O(n) time and O(1) space
Addressed the 1-indexed return requirement

modifications.question: What if the array is not sorted?
modifications.approach: Sort first for O(n log n) total, or use a HashMap for O(n). HashMap: for each element store its complement's target index. If you see a complement already stored, return the pair.

problem.text: Given an integer array nums sorted in non-decreasing order, remove duplicates in-place so each unique element appears only once. Return the count of unique elements.

keywords.explanation: duplicates are adjacent — no need for a set

missingConstraints: What should happen to elements beyond the returned count?
Is the return value the count or the array?

concepts.definition: Modifying an array without allocating a new one. Use a write pointer that advances only when a new unique value is found. The read pointer scans ahead.
concepts.example:
[1,1,2,3,3]  write=0
read=0: nums[0]=1, write: nums[0]=1, write=1
read=1: nums[1]=1 == nums[write-1], skip
read=2: nums[2]=2 != nums[write-1], write: nums[1]=2, write=2
→ [1,2,3,...]  return 3

easy_medium.text: Use a HashSet to collect unique values, copy back to array
easy_medium.explanation: O(n) time but O(n) space — violates the in-place constraint.

easy_medium.text: Two pointers: slow pointer writes unique values, fast pointer scans ahead
easy_medium.explanation: O(n) time, O(1) space. Sorted array means duplicates are adjacent — slow pointer only advances when a new unique value is found.

easy_medium.text: Sort the array, then scan for unique values
easy_medium.explanation: Array is already sorted — sorting again is O(n log n) and unnecessary.

code.sayApproach: Sorted means duplicates are adjacent — I don't need a set. I use a write pointer starting at 1, and a read pointer scanning from 1. When read finds a new unique value (different from nums[write-1]), I copy it to the write position and advance write. O(n) time, O(1) space.

lines.narration: Constraint: only write if value is new
Requirement: return count of unique elements

code.verbalSummary: Slow-fast two pointers on a sorted array. Write pointer advances only on new unique values. O(n) time, O(1) space. The sorted property guarantees duplicates are adjacent — no set needed.
solRatingCriteria: Used sorted property to avoid a HashSet
Correctly initialised write pointer at 1, not 0
Compared to nums[write-1] not nums[write]
Returned write count, not write-1

modifications.question: Allow each element to appear at most twice instead of once.
modifications.approach: Change the comparison from nums[write-1] to nums[write-2] and start write at 2. The invariant: any new element must differ from what's two positions behind the write pointer.

problem.text: Given an integer array nums, move all 0s to the end while maintaining the relative order of the non-zero elements. Do this in-place.

keywords.explanation: constraint: stable — can't just swap arbitrarily

missingConstraints: Are all elements guaranteed to be integers?

concepts.term: Stable partition with a write pointer
concepts.definition: When you need to move all elements matching a condition to one end while preserving relative order, use a write pointer for the 'keep' elements. Scan with a read pointer; copy non-matching elements to write position.

easy_medium.text: Write pointer for non-zeros, fill remaining positions with 0
easy_medium.explanation: O(n) time, O(1) space. Copy all non-zeros forward, then fill the tail with zeros. Relative order preserved.

easy_medium.text: Bubble sort-style: swap each 0 rightward until it reaches the end
easy_medium.explanation: O(n²) — each 0 might travel the entire array. Correct but far too slow.

easy_medium.text: Two passes: count zeros, rebuild array
easy_medium.explanation: O(n) time but needs O(n) space to rebuild — violates the in-place constraint.

code.sayApproach: Write pointer pattern: scan with read, copy all non-zeros to the write position. After the scan, fill everything from write to end with zeros. Two passes, O(n) time, O(1) space. Relative order preserved because we scan left-to-right.

lines.narration: Pattern: write pointer for non-zero elements
Requirement: move non-zeros forward, preserving order
Requirement: fill remaining positions with zeros

code.verbalSummary: Two-pass write-pointer approach. First pass copies all non-zeros forward preserving order. Second pass fills remaining slots with zeros. O(n) time, O(1) space.
solRatingCriteria: Used write-pointer pattern for in-place stable partition
Correctly filled tail with zeros after the scan
Stated O(n) time and O(1) space
Noted relative order is preserved by left-to-right scan

modifications.question: Instead of moving zeros to the end, move all negative numbers to the front.
modifications.approach: Same write-pointer pattern — just change the condition from != 0 to < 0. Scan, copy all negatives to write position, then fill the rest. Stable partition.

problem.text: Given an integer array nums, return all triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j, i != k, j != k, and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0. The solution must not contain duplicate triplets.

keywords.explanation: three elements — reduce to two-pointer with a fixed outer loop
constraint: skip duplicate values during iteration

missingConstraints: Can the array contain duplicate values?
Can the same index be used multiple times?

concepts.definition: Fix one element nums[i] in an outer loop. The problem becomes: find pairs in nums[i+1..n-1] that sum to -nums[i]. Since the array is sorted, use two pointers for the inner problem. This converts O(n³) brute force to O(n²).
concepts.example:
nums = [-4,-1,-1,0,1,2]  (sorted)
i=0: target = 4, two-pointer on [-1,-1,0,1,2]
i=1: target = 1, two-pointer on [-1,0,1,2]
...skip duplicate i values to avoid duplicate triplets

easy_medium.text: Sort + outer loop fixing nums[i] + two pointers on remainder
easy_medium.explanation: O(n²) time. Sort first for two-pointer to work. Skip duplicate values at each pointer to avoid duplicate triplets in the result.

easy_medium.text: HashSet of all pairs, check if third element exists
easy_medium.explanation: O(n²) time but O(n) space and tricky deduplication logic. The two-pointer approach handles deduplication more cleanly with just in-place pointer skipping.

easy_medium.text: Three nested loops checking all triplets
easy_medium.explanation: O(n³) — correct but too slow. Sorting + two pointers reduces this to O(n²).

code.sayApproach: Sort first — this enables two pointers and makes deduplication easy. Fix each element with an outer loop (skip duplicates). For each fixed element, run two pointers on the rest looking for pairs that sum to its negation. Skip duplicate pointer values to avoid duplicate triplets. O(n²) time, O(1) extra space.

lines.narration: Sort — enables two pointers and deduplication

code.verbalSummary: Sort + fix one element + two pointers for the rest. The key is deduplication: skip duplicate values at each of the three pointers. O(n²) time — sorting is O(n log n) but dominated by the O(n²) inner loop.
solRatingCriteria: Sorted the array first and justified why
Fixed outer element and ran two pointers on remainder
Handled duplicate skipping at all three levels (i, left, right)
Stated O(n²) time correctly

modifications.question: Now find all quadruplets that sum to a target.
modifications.approach: Add another outer loop, reduce to 3Sum, reduce to 2Sum. O(n³). Skip duplicates at all four levels.

problem.text: Given n non-negative integers representing the height of bars at each position, find two bars that together with the x-axis form a container that holds the most water.

keywords.explanation: two pointers — one at each end

missingConstraints: Are there guaranteed to be at least 2 bars?

concepts.definition: Always move the pointer with the shorter bar inward. Moving the taller bar inward can only decrease or maintain the width while the height is still bounded by the shorter bar — so it can never improve the area. Moving the shorter bar is the only chance to find a taller replacement.
concepts.example:
[1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7]  left=0(h=1), right=8(h=7)
Area = min(1,7) × 8 = 8
Move left (shorter): left=1(h=8)
Area = min(8,7) × 7 = 49  ← much better

easy_medium.text: Two pointers at both ends, always move the shorter bar inward
easy_medium.explanation: Greedy: moving the taller bar can never improve the area (height stays bounded by the shorter). Moving the shorter gives a chance to find a taller replacement. O(n) time.

easy_medium.text: Nested loops checking every pair of bars
easy_medium.explanation: O(n²) — the greedy insight eliminates the need to check all pairs.

easy_medium.text: Sort bars by height, use two pointers on sorted array
easy_medium.explanation: Sorting loses positional information — the width depends on the original indices, which sorting destroys.

code.sayApproach: Two pointers at both ends. Area = min(left height, right height) × width. Always move the shorter bar inward — moving the taller bar can never increase the area because height is still limited by the shorter one and width decreases. O(n) time, O(1) space.

lines.narration: Requirement: compute area of current container
Move shorter bar — only way to potentially improve
Right is shorter or equal — move it

code.verbalSummary: Greedy two pointers. Always move the shorter bar — the key insight is that moving the taller bar can never improve the area. O(n) time, O(1) space.
solRatingCriteria: Stated the area formula: min(h[l],h[r]) × (r-l)
Articulated the greedy insight for pointer movement
Stated O(n) time and O(1) space
Considered equal-height case in the else branch

modifications.question: Now find the maximum water if you can remove exactly one bar.
modifications.approach: More complex — try removing each bar and track the best. Or use left/right max prefix arrays to efficiently compute the max water when each bar is excluded. O(n) with prefix arrays.
modifications.codeChunk:
// Precompute leftMax[i] and rightMax[i]
// For each i: area without bar i uses height of neighbors

problem.text: Given a string s, return true if it is a palindrome, considering only alphanumeric characters and ignoring cases.
missingConstraints: Is an empty string a valid palindrome?
What about strings with only non-alphanumeric characters?

concepts.definition: Converge two pointers from both ends, skipping non-alphanumeric characters. Compare lowercased characters. If any pair mismatches, not a palindrome. No extra space needed — no need to build a cleaned string.
concepts.example:
"A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"
left skips to 'A', right skips to 'a'
Compare: 'a' == 'a' ✓  converge...
All pairs match → true

easy_medium.text: Clean the string first (remove non-alphanumeric, lowercase), then check with two pointers
easy_medium.explanation: O(n) time but O(n) extra space for the cleaned string. The in-place approach avoids the allocation.

easy_medium.text: Two pointers converging, skipping non-alphanumeric in-place
easy_medium.explanation: O(n) time, O(1) space. Skip non-alphanumeric at each pointer before comparing. No cleaned string needed.

easy_medium.text: Reverse the string and compare to original
easy_medium.explanation: O(n) space for the reversed string, and you still need to handle the alphanumeric/case constraints.

code.sayApproach: Two converging pointers. Skip non-alphanumeric at both ends before each comparison. Compare lowercased characters. If any pair mismatches, return false immediately. O(n) time, O(1) space — no cleaned string needed.

lines.narration: All pairs matched — it's a palindrome

code.verbalSummary: Converging two pointers with in-place alphanumeric skipping. No extra space for a cleaned string. O(n) time, O(1) space. Empty string edge case is handled — loop never executes, returns true.
solRatingCriteria: Chose in-place approach over building a cleaned string
Correctly skipped non-alphanumeric at both pointers
Used Character.isLetterOrDigit and toLowerCase
Handled empty string edge case implicitly

modifications.question: Find the longest palindromic substring.
modifications.approach: Expand around each center — O(n²). For each index i, expand outward while characters match. Also expand from between i and i+1 for even-length palindromes. Track the longest span found.

problem.text: Given n non-negative integers representing the height of bars, compute how much water can be trapped after raining.

keywords.explanation: water at position i = min(maxLeft[i], maxRight[i]) - height[i]

missingConstraints: Can all bars be zero height?

concepts.definition: The water level above bar i equals min(tallest bar to its left, tallest bar to its right) minus bar i's own height. If this is negative, no water is trapped (bar is taller than both surrounding maxima).
concepts.example:
height = [0,1,0,2,1,0,1,3,2,1,2,1]
At index 2 (h=0): leftMax=1, rightMax=3
water = min(1,3) - 0 = 1
At index 5 (h=0): leftMax=2, rightMax=3
water = min(2,3) - 0 = 2

options.text: Two pointers converging from both ends

code.sayApproach: Two pieces: traverse with two converging pointers, and track running left/right max variables instead of precomputed arrays. The key insight: if height[left] <= height[right], the water at left is determined by leftMax (rightMax is guaranteed to be >= leftMax). Process the shorter-side pointer first. O(n) time, O(1) space.

lines.narration: Process shorter side — water level determined by leftMax
Requirement: trapped water = leftMax - current height
Requirement: add right side trapped water

code.verbalSummary: Two converging pointers with running left/right max variables. Process the shorter-side pointer — when height[left] <= height[right], leftMax determines the water level at left (right side is guaranteed sufficient). O(n) time, O(1) space — eliminates the need for prefix/suffix arrays.
solRatingCriteria: Explained why the shorter-side pointer is processed first
Used running max variables instead of prefix arrays for O(1) space
Correctly computed water as max - current height
Stated O(n) time and O(1) space vs O(n) space prefix approach

modifications.question: What if the problem is 2D — given a matrix of heights, find total trapped water?
modifications.approach: Use a min-heap (priority queue) of border cells. BFS outward: for each cell, water = max(0, waterLevel - height). The waterLevel is the minimum border height that can contain it. O(m×n log(m×n)).
modifications.codeChunk:
PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a,b)->a[2]-b[2]);
// seed all border cells, BFS inward tracking water level

problem.text: Given a string s, reverse only the vowels of the string in-place.

keywords.explanation: requirement: vowels swap, consonants stay put
constraint: modify the char array directly

missingConstraints: Is matching case-sensitive — is 'A' a vowel?

concepts.definition: Two pointers converge but only swap when both point to elements matching the target condition (vowels). Skip non-matching elements by advancing the pointer without swapping.
concepts.example:
s = "hello"
left=0('h') not vowel, left++
left=1('e') vowel ✓, right=4('o') vowel ✓ → swap → "holle"
left++, right--
left=2('l') not vowel, left++
left=3 >= right=3 → done  → "holle"

options.text: Single pass collecting vowel indices, then swap

code.sayApproach: Two converging pointers. Skip non-vowels at both ends. When both point to vowels, swap them and advance both. A HashSet of vowels gives O(1) lookup. O(n) time, O(1) space (the set is fixed size).

lines.narration: Work with char array for in-place modification

code.verbalSummary: Selective two-pointer swap. Both pointers skip non-vowels before swapping. HashSet gives O(1) vowel lookup. O(n) time, O(1) space since the vowel set is constant size.
solRatingCriteria: Used a Set for O(1) vowel lookup
Handled both uppercase and lowercase vowels
Correctly advanced both pointers after each swap
Stated O(1) space for the fixed-size vowel set

modifications.question: Reverse only the consonants instead of vowels.
modifications.approach: Invert the condition — skip vowels, swap when both point to consonants. Exact same structure, different predicate.

problem.text: Given two strings s and t, return true if s is a subsequence of t.

keywords.explanation: characters appear in order but not necessarily contiguously

keywords.word: s is a subsequence of t
keywords.explanation: requirement: all chars of s appear in t in order

missingConstraints: Can s or t be empty?
Follow-up: if there are many s strings to check against the same t, how would you optimise?

concepts.definition: A subsequence preserves relative order but allows gaps. A substring requires contiguous characters. 'ace' is a subsequence of 'abcde' (gaps between a-c and c-e), but not a substring.
concepts.example:
s = "ace",  t = "abcde"
subsequence: a_c_e matches positions 0,2,4  ✓
substring:   no contiguous "ace" exists       ✗

s = "aec",  t = "abcde"
not a subsequence — 'e' appears after 'c' in t, but 'c' must come after 'e' in s  ✗

options.text: Two pointers: one on s, one on t — advance t always, advance s on match
Build LCS (longest common subsequence) and check if length == s.length
Binary search on precomputed position lists per character

code.sayApproach: Two pointers: one on s (si), one on t (ti). Advance ti always. Advance si only on a match. If si reaches s.length(), all characters of s were found in order in t. O(n) where n = t.length. For the follow-up with many queries against the same t: precompute a map of character → sorted list of positions in t, then binary search for each character of s.

lines.narration: Returns true if s is subsequence of t
Advance until one string is exhausted
Requirement: match found — advance s pointer
Requirement: all of s matched if si reached the end

code.verbalSummary: Two pointers: always advance t, advance s only on a match. O(|t|) time, O(1) space. For the follow-up with many queries: precompute position lists per character in t, binary search for each character of s — O(|s| log |t|) per query after O(|t|) preprocessing.
solRatingCriteria: Correctly advanced t always and s only on match
Returned si == s.length() not ti == t.length()
Addressed the follow-up: binary search on precomputed position lists
Stated O(|t|) time and O(1) space for the simple case

modifications.question: Follow-up: given 10^9 strings s to check against the same t, what's the optimal approach?
modifications.approach: Precompute: for each character c and position i in t, store the next occurrence of c at or after i. This is a 2D array dp[i][c] = next position of character c in t starting from index i. Build in O(26n). Each query is O(|s|).
